---
title: "Notation and Assumptions"
---

## Core Symbols

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $y, y_{1:n}$ | observed data |
| $\tilde{y}$ | future or replicated data |
| $\theta$ | parameter vector |
| $\phi$ | hyperparameter or nuisance parameter |
| $\pi(\theta)$ | prior density or mass function |
| $p(y \mid \theta)$ | likelihood |
| $p(\theta \mid y)$ | posterior |
| $p(\tilde{y} \mid y)$ | posterior predictive distribution |
| $\ell(\theta; y)$ | log-likelihood |
| $U(\theta)$ | score or potential energy, depending on context |
| $\mathcal{L}(q)$ | evidence lower bound (ELBO) |
| $\hat{R}$ | rank-normalized convergence diagnostic |
| ESS | effective sample size |

## Probability Conventions

- Densities and probability mass functions are both written with $p(\cdot)$ unless distinction is essential.
- Priors are often written as $\pi(\theta)$ to distinguish them visually from sampling densities.
- Expectations under a distribution $p$ are written $\mathbb{E}_p[\cdot]$ when ambiguity is possible.
- Conditional independence is denoted by $\perp\!\!\!\perp$.

## Modeling Conventions

- Scalar parameters use lower-case Greek letters.
- Vectors use bold Roman letters when clarity matters, for example $\mathbf{x}_i$.
- Matrices use upper-case Roman letters, for example $X$.
- Hierarchical indices follow the pattern $y_{ij}$ for unit $i$ in group $j$.

## Computational Conventions

- Unnormalized log posteriors are used frequently because MCMC and optimization often need only ratios or gradients.
- All MCMC examples assume the target density is known up to proportionality.
- Diagnostics are interpreted jointly rather than in isolation.

## Standing Assumptions

Unless otherwise stated:

- parameter spaces are measurable;
- posterior normalizing constants exist when posteriors are claimed proper;
- gradients and Hessians are assumed to exist where optimization or HMC derivations need them;
- independence claims are always model assumptions, not universal truths.
